title: Aristotle (384–322 B.C.E.) was a foundational Greek philosopher and scientist who
excerpts:
Section Title: Aristotle
Content:
HISTORY.com Editors
Pixabay/Couleur
Published: November 09, 2009 Last Updated: March 06, 2025
The Greek philosopher Aristotle (384-322 B.C.) made significant and lasting contributions to nearly every aspect of human knowledge, from logic to biology to ethics and aesthetics. Though overshadowed in classical times by the work of his teacher [Plato](https://www.history.com/topics/ancient-history/plato) , from late antiquity through [the Enlightenment,](https://www.history.com/topics/british-history/enlightenment) Aristotle’s surviving writings were incredibly influential. In Arabic philosophy, he was known simply as “The First Teacher.” In the West, he was “The Philosopher.”

title: An Introduction to Aristotle Life | Issue 13 | Philosophy Now
excerpts:
Section Title: An Introduction to Aristotle Life > Overall approach
Content:
Aristotle divided science (from the Greek for Knowledge) into 3 different areas:
*Theoretical/Speculative Science* (mathematics, physics and metaphysics), *Practical Science* (ethics, politics and economics) and *Poetical Science* (from the Greek *Poiesis* - Crafts: covering areas as diverse as drama, literature, shoemaking and medicine! To comprehend any of these you need the primary science which precedes all the rest: Logic.

title: Aristotle
excerpts:
Nicomachean Ethics * Politics * Rhetoric * Poetics 

    * Aristotle: 


Aristotle [ A ] ( Attic Greek : Ἀριστοτέλης , romanized:  Aristotélēs ; [ B ] 384–322 BC) was an Ancient Greek philosopher and polymath . His writings cover a broad range of subjects spanning the natural sciences , philosophy , linguistics , economics , politics , psychology , and the arts . As the founder of the Peripatetic school of philosophy in the Lyceum in Athens , he began the wider Aristotelian tradition that followed, which set the groundwork for the development of modern science . Little is known about Aristotle's life. He was born in the city of Stagira in northern Greece during the Classical period . His father, Nicomachus , died when Aristotle was a child, and he was brought up by a guardian. At around eighteen years old, he joined Plato 's Academy in Athens and remained there until the age of thirty seven (
c.  347 BC ).

title: Episode # 005 -  Transcript — Philosophize This!
excerpts:
Section Title: Episode # 005 - Transcript
Content:
Aristotle separates these real goods down into a few different parts. He says human beings are animals with the unique ability to think and reason. Because we’re animals with a machine-like body that needs maintenance all the time, some of these real goods help us with our bodily needs. These bodily goods, as they’re called, are made up of vigor, vitality, health, and sensual pleasures. So, Aristotle says that even though we go about getting these things in a variety of different ways, we not only share all these desires with every other human; we actually share all these desires with every other animal in the animal kingdom, because all animals avoid pain and seek pleasure as well as vigor, vitality, and health.

title: Aristotle - PHIL 2101: Introduction to Philosophy - LibGuides at Brooklyn College Library
excerpts:
Section Title: PHIL 2101: Introduction to Philosophy: Aristotle > About Aristotle
Content:
Aristotle, Greek Aristoteles, (born 384 BCE, Stagira, Chalcidice, Greece—died 322, Chalcis, Euboea), ancient Greek philosopher and scientist, one of the greatest intellectual figures of Western history. He was the author of a philosophical and scientific system that became the framework and vehicle for both Christian Scholasticism and medieval Islamic philosophy. Even after the intellectual revolutions of the Renaissance, the Reformation, and the Enlightenment, Aristotelian concepts remained embedded in Western thinking.
